plantings.variety: Super Star + Yellow Sweet Spanish (~18 each)

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Pre-fruit feed — bone meal
tasks.description: Side-dress 1 tbsp bone meal per plant once first flowers appear. Supports fruit set and meets phosphorus demand during flowering.

tasks.title: Inspect for early blight
tasks.description: Look for brown spots with yellow halos on lower leaves. Remove affected leaves immediately and dispose (do not compost). Increase airflow by pruning dense interior foliage if severe.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Inspect for early blight
tasks.description: Look for brown spots with yellow halos on lower leaves. Remove affected leaves immediately and dispose (do not compost). Increase airflow by pruning dense interior foliage if severe.

tasks.title: Mid-season feed — blood meal
tasks.description: Work 1 tbsp blood meal per plant into top inch of soil. Apply once heavy fruiting begins to compensate for nitrogen demand. Skip if foliage is already very dark green — excess nitrogen pushes leaf growth at expense of fruit.

tasks.title: Inspect for hornworms — apply Bt if found
tasks.description: Check undersides of leaves on lower third of plant. Look for dark frass (droppings) as first sign. If hornworms found: spray Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) on affected foliage per label. Hand-pick any large worms already present. Reapply Bt after rain.

tasks.title: Inspect for early blight
tasks.description: Look for brown spots with yellow halos on lower leaves. Remove affected leaves immediately and dispose (do not compost). Increase airflow by pruning dense interior foliage if severe.

tasks.title: First harvest check
tasks.description: Roma and paste types: firm and fully colored. Slicers: slight give when squeezed. Do not wait for perfect color in heat — fully vine-ripened tomatoes go soft fast in summer temperatures.

tasks.title: Check for suckers
tasks.description: Remove suckers forming in the crotch between main stem and lateral branches. Indeterminate varieties only (Amish Paste, Comstock, Bonny Best) — leave determinates (Roma, Orange Hat) unpruned. Pinch suckers when small (under 2 inches) for a cleaner break.

tasks.title: Tip new blackberry primocanes if 3–4 ft tall
tasks.description: New canes logged June 14 are now 23 days old; tip at 3–4 ft now to promote lateral branching for next year's fruit.

tasks.title: Fall renovation
tasks.description: After last harvest in fall, cut all foliage back to 1 inch above crown. Remove old mulch and debris. Add 1 inch of fresh compost to the bed. Apply fall Berry-Tone feeding at label rate. This resets the plant for next year.

tasks.title: Early spring feed — Berry-Tone
tasks.description: Apply True Organic Berry-Tone at label rate (approximately 1/2 cup per 5 sq ft of bed) as soon as new growth appears in early spring. Work lightly into top inch of soil and water in well.

logs.note: Bajillion flowers
Bajillion flowers
Bajillion flowers
Watering hard for one hour. Also added more nutrients in the form of berry food and worm castings to try and help.
Final yield only 1 lb. Delicious. I think I needed to plant a little earlier and water more generously.
Looking really rough. Added fertilizer and watered full hose for 1 hour to try and kinda bring it back but she is a sad little tree this year.
4 sprouted. 2 knocked back by a late frost but I think they will come back
Sprouts! Sprouted today already a couple inches tall. Six of the 8 so far.
Pods observed. Lots already
Pods beginning to form
Pods just starting to appear
Pulled weeds that grew through the straw
The weird little pea tentacles have shown up
The weird little pea tentacles have shown up
The weird little pea tentacles have shown up
Added a top dress of worm castings. Approx one or two cups.
Added a top dress of worm castings. Approx one or two cups.
Added a top dress of worm castings. Approx one or two cups.
Added a top dress of worm castings. Approx one or two cups.
Planted.
Installed bed. 2 large bags of Kellogg organic potting soil mix. Bag of chicken compost. Healthy serving of bone meal, worm castings, humate and a berry mix organic fertilizer
Installed bed today. Two bags of Kellogg organic raised bed mix. One bag of chicken compost. Humate and blood meal and worm castings.
First sprout observed
Sprouts observed
Did a little thinning
Watered today. Wide spread sprouting observed after a warm day of sun
Watered. First sprouts observed!
Sprouts noticed today!
Watered today. Also installed the trellis arch and frost cloth.
Watered today. Also installed the trellis arch and frost cloth.
Watered today. Also installed the trellis arch and frost cloth.
First sprout observed
Friggin quail did their dirt baths in this bed. Possible some beans lost they really wallowed in there. So obnoxious
First bean sprout appeared!
First sprout noticed
Yeah I think she’s dead. Gonna water one more time to be sure
Couldn’t find my watering can so I applied berry food instead of fish emulsion
All five sprouted so the two that froze bounced back. I hill d 2 of them today
Applied some berry food
2 bags full. Hilled the other 3. The other 3 probably have 1 more hilling and they will be full.
Berries observed. Also at least five new canes
All five bounced back. Hurried leaves and rolled out the bags a bit more today. I would say on the two that didn’t freeze we are 2/3 done and the others about 1/3 done

one potato died, i think it was the russet. not sure what happened but its toast. started over with a cutting from another potato, will see how it goes. all the other bags are basically hilled and are now in growth mode.
Fish emulsion applied
Fish emulsion applied
Fish emulsion applied

About 12 oz of radish is all. After tops cut.

payload.headline: Beans hit 55 days — pick before afternoon storms.
weather_notes: Run zones before noon today — 3 days dry at 93°F exceeds the 1-day heat threshold; skip only if today's 60% afternoon storms actually deliver rain.
Harvest beans (c2/c3/c4) and check blackberries before noon — today's 60% afternoon storm window will knock ripe pods and bruise cane fruit.
Hold all foliar feeds and sprays until Thursday morning — today's 60% afternoon storms will wash off applications before they absorb.
Plan a zone run for Thursday morning — 95-99°F with no rain forecast Thursday-Friday will push soil past the re-water threshold within a day of today's storms clearing.

suggested_tasks.description: Jade Bush (c3/c4) and Red Swan (c2) hit 55 days today — pick this morning before today's 60% afternoon storms knock ripe pods.

suggested_tasks.title: Feed w1 strawberries with berry food
suggested_tasks.description: 49 days since the Jun 3 berry food application — apply Friday morning after today's storms clear and soil surface dries slightly.

payload.headline: Beans at peak — harvest this morning before storms hit.
weather_notes: Run both zones this morning — 2 days dry at 99°F is past the 1-day heat threshold; hold the next cycle if Wednesday's 60% storms actually deliver.
Pick beans and blackberries this morning — tonight's 50% and Wednesday's 60% storm chances risk knocking pods and bruising ripe fruit on the cane.
Apply fish emulsion to e1–e4 before noon — Wednesday's 60% storm window will help wash it into root zones.
Finish all outdoor tasks before noon — 99°F heat and afternoon thunderstorms close the outdoor work window through Wednesday.

suggested_tasks.title: Feed e1–e4 tomatoes with fish emulsion

Skip zone irrigation today and tomorrow — watered yesterday; hold to see if Tuesday's 50% storm chance delivers first meaningful rain.
Harvest beans and blackberries this morning — 100°F softens pods and damages ripe fruit on the cane by afternoon.
Hold all foliar sprays and feeds until Thursday — 50–70% storm chances Tuesday through Wednesday will wash off same-day applications.
Check e1–e4 transplants after Tuesday's storms — 30-day plants risk stem lodging from heavy monsoon downpours.

Check e1–e4 transplants Wednesday morning — first 96°F window after 4+ days above 100°F; pull any heat-damaged foliage before it invites disease.
Monitor new potato bag cutting daily — just 4 days since the Jul 9 restart, most vulnerable plant in continued 100°F+ heat.

payload.headline: Extreme heat incoming; water deeply and protect
weather_notes: An Extreme Heat Warning is active until Tuesday morning, with daily highs expected to exceed 100F.
No precipitation is forecast through Wednesday, necessitating close attention to irrigation.

suggested_tasks.title: Tip new blackberry primocanes
suggested_tasks.description: This overdue task encourages lateral branching for increased fruit production next year.

suggested_tasks.title: Deeply water all beds
suggested_tasks.description: Daily deep watering is crucial for plant survival and productivity during extreme triple-digit heat.

suggested_tasks.title: Install shade cloth
suggested_tasks.description: Protect young tomato and bean plants from scorching and heat stress in the extreme temperatures.

payload.headline: 106°F today — zone 1 three days dry.
weather_notes: Run zone 1 immediately — e1–e4 transplants and c-beds are 3 days dry entering 106°F today, two days past the 1-day re-water threshold for heat above 85°F.
Do all garden work before 8 AM through Tuesday — Extreme Heat Warning is active until July 14 with 103–106°F daily highs closing every outdoor window.
Hang shade cloth over e1–e4 by midday — 22-day tomato transplants risk permanent sunscald at 106°F; remove by 5 PM.
Check potato bag moisture after zone 1 runs — bags lose moisture 2–3× faster than in-ground beds at 106°F.

payload.headline: 107°F Sunday — run zones now, shade young tomatoes.
weather_notes: Run both zones this morning — 2 days dry entering today's 103°F crosses the 1-day re-water threshold for heat above 85°F.
Do all outdoor garden work before 8 AM today through Monday — Extreme Heat Warning is active through July 14 with 103–107°F highs.
Hang shade cloth over e1–e4 midday Sunday — 107°F risks permanent sunscald on 21-day transplants; remove by 5 PM.
Check the new potato-bag cutting daily through Monday — started July 9 and not yet established, making it the highest-risk plant in Sunday's 107°F.

payload.headline: Extreme heat peaks 106°F — tip canes now, zones Saturday.
weather_notes: Do all garden work before 8 AM through Monday — Extreme Heat Warning is active until July 14 with 100–106°F daily highs.
Run both zones early Saturday morning — yesterday's irrigation is 2 days dry by Saturday's 103°F, crossing the re-water threshold for extreme heat.
Check e1–e4 transplant soil between irrigation cycles — 20-day tomatoes under 100–106°F may need spot watering before the next scheduled zone run.
Check potato bag moisture daily through Monday — bags lose moisture 2–3× faster than ground beds in 100–106°F extreme heat.
